title: Fault display mapping: System vs AGV faults
summary: Faults are split: general system faults show on the left, AGV-related faults on the right. Example: if an AGV is commanded to move and no in‑progress/movement occurs, it appears on the right; communication-loss type items appear on the left.
observed_evidence: So if we tell an AGB to move and we haven't gotten in progress... it's a system fault...
On the left, you'll see system faults and on the right there's AGV system faults.
If we told an AGV to move but it hasn't moved, it'll be on the right.
relationships: Loss of communication -> listed under system faults (left panel).
AGV command not starting -> listed under AGV system faults (right panel).
Screen organizes faults by scope (system-wide vs AGV-specific).
retrieval_text: Fault screen: left = system faults (e.g., comms loss); right = AGV system faults. If an AGV is told to move but no in-progress/movement, it shows on the right.

title: Remote monitoring constraints and workarounds (RMS/Ignition)
summary: Thin-client environments limit remote visibility of RMS screens. Current workaround is to pull data via APIs or query the database to mirror on-screen info. Long term, replicating these views in Ignition is desired for easier remote access.
observed_evidence: Because they're on thin clients, we can't see our mess.
We're kind of dependent on either pulling an API...
…or using the database to see what's on their screen.
relationships: Thin clients -> restrict direct RMS screen sharing.
APIs/DB -> used to mirror RMS screen data for remote support.
Desired: replicate RMS views in Ignition for easier access.
retrieval_text: Remote RMS visibility is limited on thin clients; teams pull via API or database to see what’s on-screen. Goal: replicate views in Ignition for easier remote access.

title: Hot bins definition and urgency context
summary: Hot bins are highest-priority bins/totes requiring fast processing, e.g., when a trailer departs in ~10 minutes, those must be completed first.
observed_evidence: 02:17:19: "Hot bins are the highest priority bins."
02:17:23: "the ones that I need to get [done] fast."
02:17:31: Trailer leaving in 10 minutes; "I need those done."
relationships: tote -> may be designated as 'hot' when outbound deadline is imminent
hot bin -> implies expedited processing
priority status -> driven by operational deadlines
retrieval_text: Hot bins: the highest-priority bins/totes processed urgently, especially before outbound trailer pulls (e.g., 10-minute window).

title: Priority signaling via 'indicator' messages (hop in/hop in close)
summary: Hot status is signaled via API/socket 'indicator' messages ('hop in'/'hop in close'). These messages are exchanged to mark what is hot for prioritization.
observed_evidence: 02:17:40: "they send us a message saying what's [hot]" / "what's hopped in"
02:17:50: "once they hit hop in close or hop in..."; "message type is called the indicator"
02:18:10: "all this stuff is through APIs"
relationships: indicator messages ('hop in'/'hop in close') -> mark hot items
APIs/socket -> transport indicator messages
hot flag -> informs tote/bin prioritization
retrieval_text: Hot designation is communicated via API/socket as 'indicator' messages: hop in/hop in close. These exchanged signals indicate which totes/bins are hot for prioritization.

title: Tote status fields and exchange readiness indicators
summary: Tote status view shows init reason, current task, exchange type/priority, readiness for exchange, whether an AUV is behind it, station, sorter task UID, task name (e.g., pickup tote), errors, and tote ID.
observed_evidence: tote status of why it wasn't initialized
shows the current task and what kind of exchange type ... if it's ready for exchange
if there's an AUV behind it ... what stations it's at ... sorter task UID ... any error ... the tote ID
relationships: tote -> station: location displayed
tote -> task: current task and exchange type
tote -> error: visible if present
retrieval_text: Tote status view includes: init reason, current task, exchange type/priority, exchange readiness, AUV behind, station, sorter task UID, task name (pickup tote), errors, tote ID.

title: Exchange thresholds and calculation methods
summary: Exchange settings use thresholds for queue, pickup, priority, and close (e.g., 60/70/80%). Methods can be counts, cube, or a mix. Customers can update, but values are typically finalized with the UPS project team at the end of UAT.
observed_evidence: you have a queue, a pick up, a priority and a close ... sixty 70 80%
software has the ability to do it based off counts ... method is either counts cube or cube and counts
updated by the customer ... finalize it with the project team with UPS at the end of UAT
relationships: thresholds -> methods: counts | cube | combined
thresholds -> stages: queue | pickup | priority | close
governance -> thresholds: customer update; finalized end of UAT
retrieval_text: Exchange thresholds: queue, pickup, priority, close (e.g., 60/70/80%). Methods: counts, cube, or both. Customer can update; typically finalized with UPS project team at end of UAT.

title: Aviva access restrictions and UPS-mediated communication
summary: Fortna support lacks direct Aviva access; only UPS users can access. PC does not communicate with Aviva. Coordinate with a UPS operator to obtain Aviva observations during troubleshooting.
observed_evidence: “We don't have the access to Aviva.”
“anybody ... not a UPS ... doesn't have access to Aviva.”
“PC doesn't talk to Aviva.”
relationships: requires: UPS operator to relay Aviva status
excludes: direct Fortna/PC access to Aviva
boundary: PC and Aviva are separate systems
retrieval_text: No direct Aviva access for Fortna/PC; only UPS users. For Aviva details, work through a UPS operator. PC does not communicate with Aviva.

title: First-response diagnostic step: check RMS system faults
summary: On any issue, first open the RMS screen and check for system faults; this is the first step during a support call before deeper triage.
observed_evidence: “first thing first, go to that RMS screen”
“see if any system faults.”
“This should always be your first step on a phone call.”
relationships: first_step: use RMS faults before deeper triage
supports: phone triage workflow
independent_of: direct Aviva access
retrieval_text: Initial triage: open RMS screen and check for system faults before escalating or requesting Aviva checks.

title: Support should prevent recurrence of known issues
summary: Speaker asserts recurring issues are a problem and should not keep happening; known issues should be prevented from recurring by support.
observed_evidence: “recurring issues are a problem”
“Recurring issues should not.”
“the same issues shouldn't happen again and again”
retrieval_text: Recurring issues are a problem; known issues should not happen again and again. Support is expected to prevent repeats.
